AUT.name: Austria
AUT.note: EU member state; EU Taxonomy Regulation (EU 2020/852) applies directly.
features: Defines which economic activities count as environmentally sustainable, under the EU Taxonomy Regulation (EU 2020/852).

An activity must substantially contribute to at least one of six environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, sustainable use/protection of water and marine resources, transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and protection/restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems.
Must comply with the "Do No Significant Harm" (DNSH) principle toward the other five objectives.
Must meet Minimum Social Safeguards (human rights, labour rights, responsible business conduct).
Must meet Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) set by the European Commission.
Distinguishes "taxonomy-eligible" activities (covered by the taxonomy) from "taxonomy-aligned" activities (which meet all criteria).
Large companies covered by the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) must disclose their taxonomy eligibility and alignment.
Not a mandatory investment list, not a "greenness" rating of companies, and not a judgement of financial performance — activities excluded from the list are not automatically "polluting".

facts.mandatory: Mandatory disclosure for large companies under the CSRD

fullDescription: The EU Taxonomy Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2020/852) entered into force on 12 July 2020 and applies directly in all EU member states, including Austria — it does not need to be separately transposed into national law the way a directive would. [1] It sets out four overarching conditions an economic activity must meet to qualify as environmentally sustainable: it must make a substantial contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, do no significant harm (DNSH) to the other five, comply with minimum social safeguards (covering human rights, labour rights and business conduct), and meet technical screening criteria (TSC) set out in delegated acts. [1]

The six environmental objectives are climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, the sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources, the transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and the protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems. The first two objectives were covered by the Climate Delegated Act (in force from January 2022), later joined by a Complementary Climate Delegated Act (from January 2023) that added strict, conditional criteria for specific nuclear and natural gas activities as transitional bridge technologies. The remaining four objectives were added by the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024). [2]

Large companies operating in Austria, as elsewhere in the EU, must disclose under Article 8 of the Regulation what share of their turnover, capital expenditure and operating expenditure is 'taxonomy-eligible' (covered by the taxonomy at all) versus 'taxonomy-aligned' (actually meeting all four conditions) — this sits alongside broader sustainability reporting obligations under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). The Commission has been actively simplifying the framework: a Delegated Act reducing reporting burden was adopted in 2025 as part of the 'Omnibus' simplification package, [3] and in March 2026 the Commission proposed further revisions to the Climate and Environmental Delegated Acts' technical screening criteria, with the revised criteria expected to apply from January 2027. [4]

activityList.criteria: Electricity, heat and gas generation must meet a life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions threshold under the Climate Delegated Act; solar and wind generation are automatically recognised, while specific nuclear and natural gas activities can qualify only under strict conditions introduced by the Complementary Climate Delegated Act (e.g. permitting deadlines and replacement of higher-emitting capacity).
activityList.threshold: Emissions thresholds set out in Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2139, as revised
activityList.dnsh: Applies to water, circular economy, pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: New construction must qualify as a Nearly Zero-Energy Building (NZEB) with primary energy demand below the applicable national NZEB threshold; major renovations must meet national building-renovation requirements or deliver substantial energy savings; individual renovation measures (insulation, efficient heating, renewables) can also qualify.
activityList.threshold: NZEB and renovation thresholds under the Climate Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to water and circular economy objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers low- and zero-emission vehicles, rail transport, and supporting infrastructure (e.g. charging points); passenger car criteria tighten over time towards a zero direct-emissions requirement.
activityList.threshold: Vehicle and infrastructure criteria under the Climate Delegated Act

activityList.criteria: Covers manufacture of low-carbon technologies and components (e.g. batteries, hydrogen equipment, energy-efficiency equipment) as well as emissions-intensive processes (cement, steel, chemicals, aluminium) assessed against sector-specific benchmarks.
activityList.threshold: Sector benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act, often referencing EU ETS best-performer benchmarks

activityList.criteria: Covers water collection, treatment and supply infrastructure, and wastewater treatment, assessed for water-efficiency and leakage performance and contribution to the water and marine resources objective.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers farming practices and land use that substantially contribute to biodiversity protection, circular economy or pollution-prevention objectives, added later than the core climate criteria.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024)

activityList.criteria: Covers data centres (assessed against energy- and resource-efficiency benchmarks broadly aligned with the EU Code of Conduct for Data Centre Energy Efficiency) and digital solutions that enable measurable GHG reductions in other sectors.
activityList.threshold: Efficiency benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act

[1] It sets out four overarching conditions an economic activity must meet to qualify as environmentally sustainable: it must make a substantial contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, do no significant harm (DNSH) to the other five, comply with minimum social safeguards (covering human rights, labour rights and business conduct), and meet technical screening criteria (TSC) set out in delegated acts. [1]

The six environmental objectives are climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, the sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources, the transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and the protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems. The first two objectives were covered by the Climate Delegated Act (in force from January 2022), later joined by a Complementary Climate Delegated Act (from January 2023) that added strict, conditional criteria for specific nuclear and natural gas activities as transitional bridge technologies. The remaining four objectives were added by the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024). [2]

Large companies operating in Germany, as elsewhere in the EU, must disclose under Article 8 of the Regulation what share of their turnover, capital expenditure and operating expenditure is 'taxonomy-eligible' (covered by the taxonomy at all) versus 'taxonomy-aligned' (actually meeting all four conditions) — this sits alongside broader sustainability reporting obligations under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). The Commission has been actively simplifying the framework: a Delegated Act reducing reporting burden was adopted in 2025 as part of the 'Omnibus' simplification package, [3] and in March 2026 the Commission proposed further revisions to the Climate and Environmental Delegated Acts' technical screening criteria, with the revised criteria expected to apply from January 2027. [4]

activityList.criteria: Electricity, heat and gas generation must meet a life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions threshold under the Climate Delegated Act; solar and wind generation are automatically recognised, while specific nuclear and natural gas activities can qualify only under strict conditions introduced by the Complementary Climate Delegated Act (e.g. permitting deadlines and replacement of higher-emitting capacity).
activityList.threshold: Emissions thresholds set out in Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2139, as revised
activityList.dnsh: Applies to water, circular economy, pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: New construction must qualify as a Nearly Zero-Energy Building (NZEB) with primary energy demand below the applicable national NZEB threshold; major renovations must meet national building-renovation requirements or deliver substantial energy savings; individual renovation measures (insulation, efficient heating, renewables) can also qualify.
activityList.threshold: NZEB and renovation thresholds under the Climate Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to water and circular economy objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers low- and zero-emission vehicles, rail transport, and supporting infrastructure (e.g. charging points); passenger car criteria tighten over time towards a zero direct-emissions requirement.
activityList.threshold: Vehicle and infrastructure criteria under the Climate Delegated Act

activityList.criteria: Covers manufacture of low-carbon technologies and components (e.g. batteries, hydrogen equipment, energy-efficiency equipment) as well as emissions-intensive processes (cement, steel, chemicals, aluminium) assessed against sector-specific benchmarks.
activityList.threshold: Sector benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act, often referencing EU ETS best-performer benchmarks

activityList.criteria: Covers water collection, treatment and supply infrastructure, and wastewater treatment, assessed for water-efficiency and leakage performance and contribution to the water and marine resources objective.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers farming practices and land use that substantially contribute to biodiversity protection, circular economy or pollution-prevention objectives, added later than the core climate criteria.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024)

activityList.criteria: Covers data centres (assessed against energy- and resource-efficiency benchmarks broadly aligned with the EU Code of Conduct for Data Centre Energy Efficiency) and digital solutions that enable measurable GHG reductions in other sectors.
activityList.threshold: Efficiency benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act

SWE.name: Sweden
SWE.note: EU member state; EU Taxonomy Regulation (EU 2020/852) applies directly.
features: Defines which economic activities count as environmentally sustainable, under the EU Taxonomy Regulation (EU 2020/852).

An activity must substantially contribute to at least one of six environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, sustainable use/protection of water and marine resources, transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and protection/restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems.
Must comply with the "Do No Significant Harm" (DNSH) principle toward the other five objectives.
Must meet Minimum Social Safeguards (human rights, labour rights, responsible business conduct).
Must meet Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) set by the European Commission.
Distinguishes "taxonomy-eligible" activities (covered by the taxonomy) from "taxonomy-aligned" activities (which meet all criteria).
Large companies covered by the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) must disclose their taxonomy eligibility and alignment.
Not a mandatory investment list, not a "greenness" rating of companies, and not a judgement of financial performance — activities excluded from the list are not automatically "polluting".

facts.mandatory: Mandatory disclosure for large companies under the CSRD

fullDescription: The EU Taxonomy Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2020/852) entered into force on 12 July 2020 and applies directly in all EU member states, including Sweden — it does not need to be separately transposed into national law the way a directive would. [1] It sets out four overarching conditions an economic activity must meet to qualify as environmentally sustainable: it must make a substantial contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, do no significant harm (DNSH) to the other five, comply with minimum social safeguards (covering human rights, labour rights and business conduct), and meet technical screening criteria (TSC) set out in delegated acts. [1]

The six environmental objectives are climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, the sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources, the transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and the protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems. The first two objectives were covered by the Climate Delegated Act (in force from January 2022), later joined by a Complementary Climate Delegated Act (from January 2023) that added strict, conditional criteria for specific nuclear and natural gas activities as transitional bridge technologies. The remaining four objectives were added by the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024). [2]

Large companies operating in Sweden, as elsewhere in the EU, must disclose under Article 8 of the Regulation what share of their turnover, capital expenditure and operating expenditure is 'taxonomy-eligible' (covered by the taxonomy at all) versus 'taxonomy-aligned' (actually meeting all four conditions) — this sits alongside broader sustainability reporting obligations under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). The Commission has been actively simplifying the framework: a Delegated Act reducing reporting burden was adopted in 2025 as part of the 'Omnibus' simplification package, [3] and in March 2026 the Commission proposed further revisions to the Climate and Environmental Delegated Acts' technical screening criteria, with the revised criteria expected to apply from January 2027. [4]

KOR.name: South Korea
KOR.note: Established by the Ministry of Environment (now Ministry of Climate, Energy and Environment) in December 2021. Based on Substantial Contribution, DNSH and Minimum Safeguards; originally 69 activities (64 Green, 5 Transitional), expanded by a December 2024 amendment.
features: Established by the Ministry of Environment (renamed Ministry of Climate, Energy and Environment in October 2025) in December 2021 to support South Korea's 2050 carbon-neutrality goal. [1][5]
Based on three principles: Substantial Contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, Do No Significant Harm (DNSH), and Minimum Safeguards (MS).
Six environmental objectives: greenhouse gas reduction, climate change adaptation, sustainable water management, circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and biodiversity conservation.

Two categories, originally covering 69 activities in the 2021 guideline: a Green area (64 activities that directly contribute to carbon neutrality) and a Transitional area (5 activities, e.g. nuclear power and LNG-related activities, considered necessary bridges toward a low-carbon economy); a December 2024 amendment added 10 further green activities. [1][2]

Alignment requires passing a four-step Alignment Assessment Process: Activity Criteria → Recognition Criteria (technical thresholds) → Exclusion Criteria (DNSH) → Protection Criteria (Minimum Safeguards).
Widely used for green bonds, green loans, green asset-backed securities, sustainable investment funds, and ESG disclosures.

fullDescription: South Korea's K-Taxonomy (Korean Green Taxonomy) was established by the (then) Ministry of Environment in December 2021, as part of the country's push toward its 2050 carbon-neutrality goal. [1] It is built on the same three core principles used by most major taxonomies: an activity must make a Substantial Contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, satisfy Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) toward the others, and meet Minimum Safeguards on social and governance conduct.

The six environmental objectives are greenhouse gas reduction, climate change adaptation, sustainable water management, transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and biodiversity conservation. Activities were originally organised into two areas under the 2021 guideline: a Green area (64 activities that directly and substantially contribute to carbon neutrality, spanning energy, transport, manufacturing, water and buildings) and a smaller Transitional area (5 activities, including specific nuclear power and LNG-related activities, treated as bridge technologies toward a low-carbon economy rather than end-state green activities). [1][4]

Alignment is assessed through a four-step process: Activity Criteria (is the activity on the list at all), Recognition Criteria (does it meet the technical/quantitative thresholds), Exclusion Criteria (does it avoid significant harm to the other objectives), and Protection Criteria (does it meet minimum safeguards). In December 2024, the guidelines were updated to add 10 new eligible activities and revise 21 existing ones — particularly around water conservation, the circular economy, pollution control and biodiversity — introducing 25 new and 6 updated technical criteria. [2] The K-Taxonomy is used mainly for green bond and green loan verification, and since December 2024 also underpins Green Credit Management Guidelines applied to bank lending. [2]

features: Regional framework covering all 10 ASEAN member states, developed by the ASEAN Taxonomy Board.
Built around the principle of a "just and orderly transition" — allows countries to follow different pathways based on their own economic circumstances.
Four environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, protection of healthy ecosystems and biodiversity, and resource resilience/transition to a circular economy.
Uses a traffic-light classification: Green (substantial contribution), Amber (credible transition pathway), Red (not aligned / significant harm).

fullDescription: Brunei is one of the ten ASEAN member states covered by the ASEAN Taxonomy for Sustainable Finance, a regional framework developed by the ASEAN Taxonomy Board rather than a purely national one. [1] No separate national green taxonomy for Brunei has been identified; the country's monetary authority, Autoriti Monetari Brunei Darussalam, has instead focused on sustainable finance instruments such as green sukuk rather than publishing its own classification framework. [2] The regional taxonomy is explicitly built around the principle of a 'just and orderly transition' — recognising that member states are at very different stages of economic development and allowing each country flexibility in how it applies the framework, rather than imposing a single uniform pathway.

The taxonomy defines four environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, protection of healthy ecosystems and biodiversity, and resource resilience/transition to a circular economy. It uses a distinctive traffic-light classification instead of a simple pass/fail test: activities are graded Green (substantial contribution), Amber (a credible transition pathway, not yet fully aligned) or Red (not aligned, or causing significant harm). [1][3]

Two assessment approaches sit underneath this: the Foundation Framework, a qualitative, principles-based checklist intended to be accessible to a wide range of users, and the more rigorous Plus Standard, which applies quantitative technical screening criteria. [3] Distinctively, the framework includes 'Remedial Measures to Transition' as one of its Essential Criteria — an activity causing some harm can still qualify if a credible plan exists to eliminate that harm within five years. [4] The ASEAN Taxonomy was designed with interoperability with the EU Taxonomy in mind, though as a regional reference framework it is voluntary rather than a mandatory national disclosure regime.

overlays.name: ASEAN Taxonomy for Sustainable Finance
overlays.scope: Regional (10 ASEAN member states)
features: Regional framework covering all 10 ASEAN member states, developed by the ASEAN Taxonomy Board.
Built around the principle of a "just and orderly transition" — allows countries to follow different pathways based on their own economic circumstances.
Four environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, protection of healthy ecosystems and biodiversity, and resource resilience/transition to a circular economy.
Uses a traffic-light classification: Green (substantial contribution), Amber (credible transition pathway), Red (not aligned / significant harm).

Two assessment approaches: the Foundation Framework (qualitative, principles-based) and the more rigorous Plus Standard (quantitative technical screening criteria, split into Tier 1 Green, Tier 2 Amber and Tier 3 Amber).
Allows "remedial measures to transition": an activity causing harm can still qualify if a credible plan exists to eliminate that harm within 5 years.
A regional framework, not a national taxonomy — several member states also maintain their own national taxonomies alongside it.

CHN.taxonomy: Green Finance Project Catalogue (2025) — formerly the Green Bond Endorsed Project Catalogue (2021)
CHN.note: Unified national catalogue jointly issued by the PBOC, National Financial Regulatory Administration and CSRC, merging the former green bond and green loan catalogues into one standard, effective 1 Oct 2025. officialDocuments.title: People's Bank of China — Green Bond Endorsed Project Catalogue (2021 Edition)
PBOC / National Financial Regulatory Administration / CSRC — Green Finance Project Catalogue (2025 edition)

features: Issued jointly by the People's Bank of China (PBOC), the National Financial Regulatory Administration and the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC); the 2025 edition merges what were previously separate green bond and green loan catalogues into one unified national standard. [1]

Covers seven broad categories: energy conservation and carbon reduction, environmental protection, resource recycling, green and low-carbon energy transition, ecological protection and restoration, green infrastructure upgrades, and green services and trade. [1]

Supersedes the 2021 Green Bond Endorsed Project Catalogue, itself China's first unified green bond standard, which organised 204 specific eligible project types under 6 main categories and 21 subcategories. [2][3]
The 2025 edition expands eligible activities to include digital/IT services and smart manufacturing, and takes effect 1 October 2025. [1][4]
Used as the reference standard for green bond and green loan eligibility in China's domestic financial market, rather than as a mandatory economy-wide disclosure regime.

Unlike EU- or Korea-style taxonomies, it does not use a formally codified Do No Significant Harm or Minimum Safeguards test — eligibility is determined by a project's inclusion in the listed categories.

fullDescription: China's green finance classification system was substantially consolidated in 2025: the People's Bank of China (PBOC), the National Financial Regulatory Administration and the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) jointly issued a unified Green Finance Project Catalogue that, for the first time, merges what had previously been separate green bond and green loan catalogues into a single national standard, effective 1 October 2025. [1]

The 2025 Catalogue organises eligible projects into seven broad categories: energy conservation and carbon reduction, environmental protection, resource recycling, green and low-carbon energy transition, ecological protection and restoration, green infrastructure upgrades, and green services and trade — the update also expands eligible activities to include digital/IT services and smart manufacturing. [1]

It supersedes the Green Bond Endorsed Project Catalogue (2021 Edition), China's first unified green bond standard, jointly issued by the PBOC, the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) and the CSRC in April 2021 and effective from July 2021. That catalogue organised 204 specific eligible project types under 6 main categories and 21 subcategories, and notably removed 'clean utilisation of coal' from the eligible list. [2][3] Unlike the EU or Korean taxonomies, China's framework does not use a formally codified Do No Significant Harm or Minimum Safeguards test — eligibility is determined by a project's inclusion in the listed categories — and it is used primarily as the reference standard for green bond and green loan issuance rather than as an economy-wide mandatory disclosure regime.

AUS.name: Australia
AUS.note: Voluntary classification (Green/Transition) focused on climate change mitigation and transition finance.

officialDocuments.title: Australian Sustainable Finance Institute — Australian Sustainable Finance Taxonomy (official page)

features: A voluntary classification system (not a mandatory regulatory requirement), developed by the Australian Sustainable Finance Institute (ASFI) in partnership with the Commonwealth Treasury. [1]
Version 1 was released on 17 June 2025, focused on climate change mitigation, with a strong emphasis on transition finance for emissions-intensive sectors. [1]

Sets technical screening criteria across six priority sectors, plus Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) criteria for other environmental objectives and Minimum Social Safeguards (MSS) covering corporate governance, human rights and First Nations peoples' rights. [1][2]
Classifies activities as "Green" (already aligned with a 1.5°C pathway) or "Transition" (contributing to decarbonisation but not yet fully aligned). [1]

Covers hard-to-abate sectors reflecting Australia's economic structure, including mining (lithium, copper, nickel, iron ore), agriculture, and transport (passenger, freight, rail, maritime, aviation). [2]
Intended to support sustainable investment product labelling, green and transition finance, corporate disclosures, and credible transition planning.

Australian Sustainable Finance Taxonomy Version 1 released

fullDescription: The Australian Sustainable Finance Taxonomy is a voluntary classification system, with Version 1 released on 17 June 2025 by the Australian Sustainable Finance Institute (ASFI) in partnership with the Commonwealth Treasury, rather than as a government-mandated regulatory requirement. [1] It currently focuses on climate change mitigation, with particular emphasis on transition finance for emissions-intensive sectors of the Australian economy (such as mining, agriculture and heavy industry) that need credible pathways to decarbonise rather than being excluded outright. [2]

To be taxonomy-aligned, an activity must meet three conditions: Technical Screening Criteria across six priority sectors, Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) safeguards for other environmental objectives, and Minimum Social Safeguards (MSS) covering corporate governance, human rights and First Nations peoples' rights. [1][2] Activities are classified as either 'Green' (already aligned with a 1.5°C pathway) or 'Transition' (contributing meaningfully to decarbonisation but not yet fully aligned) — a two-tier structure designed to bring emissions-intensive but transitioning activities into the scope of sustainable finance rather than leaving them entirely outside it. [1]

The taxonomy followed an extended scoping and development process: an ASFI-led scoping project ran from June 2022 to July 2023 (reviewing international taxonomies and recommending Australia's design elements), followed by a development phase from July 2023 to February 2025 involving broad-based collaboration and public consultation over twenty months. [3] Because it was developed independently of the EU framework, the Australian taxonomy is not legally interoperable with the EU Taxonomy, but it was designed with broadly similar principles (substantial contribution, DNSH, safeguards) to support cross-border comparability for investors.

fullDescription: On 31 October 2025, Brazil's federal government published Decree No. 12,705/2025, formally establishing the Brazilian Sustainable Taxonomy (Taxonomia Sustentável Brasileira, TSB) as an instrument of the federal Ecological Transformation Plan. [1][2] Its strategic objective is to reduce information asymmetries and strengthen the integrity of the sustainable finance market while ensuring interoperability with international standards.

The TSB defines environmental objectives covering climate change mitigation and adaptation, biodiversity and water resource conservation, pollution prevention and control, and the transition to a circular economy; activities must also meet Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) criteria against the remaining objectives, alongside cross-cutting minimum social, environmental and governance safeguards that apply regardless of an activity's technical environmental performance. Distinctively, it also incorporates social goals such as reducing racial and gender inequalities, going beyond the environment-only scope of most other taxonomies. [1][3]

The first edition is organised into eight sectoral notebooks: agriculture, livestock, forestry, fishing and aquaculture; extractive industries; manufacturing industry; electricity and gas; water, sewage, waste and decontamination; construction; transport, storage and postal services; and social services, tourism, urban planning and ICT. This followed a draft published for public consultation in December 2024 and technical reports approved by the Interinstitutional Committee (CITSB) in August 2025. [1][2]

MEX.regulator: Secretariat of Finance and Public Credit (SHCP)
fullDescription: On 16 March 2023, Mexico's Secretariat of Finance and Public Credit (SHCP) issued the first edition of the Sustainable Taxonomy of Mexico, created to provide a reliable, legitimate, unified and science-based classification system for determining which economic activities can be considered sustainable. [1][2]

The Taxonomy is distinctive worldwide for explicitly incorporating social objectives alongside environmental ones: it defines three overarching goals — climate change, gender equality, and access to basic services related to sustainable cities — alongside more specific environmental objectives covering climate change mitigation and adaptation, sustainable management of water and marine resources, biodiversity and ecosystem conservation, the transition to a circular economy, and pollution prevention and control. Social objectives include contributing to gender equality, access to basic services in sustainable cities, promotion of health and education, and financial inclusion. [1][2]

The Taxonomy covers 124 specific activities distributed across six economic sectors: agriculture and forestry, energy and water, manufacturing, transport, construction, and waste management. It is considered the first taxonomy in the world to define gender equality as a priority objective in its design, reflecting an approach broader than the environment-only scope of most other national taxonomies. [1][2]

fullDescription: El Salvador presented its Green Taxonomy in December 2025, a technical framework classifying economic activities that make a substantial contribution to the country's environmental and climate objectives, intended to guide capital flows and facilitate access to international green financing markets. [1]

It was built through a governance structure between the Superintendencia del Sistema Financiero (SSF, a member of the Central American Council of Superintendents), the Central Reserve Bank, and the Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources, with technical support from the International Finance Corporation (IFC), financial support from the Government of Spain, and private-sector participation through FUNDEMAS. [1]

The Taxonomy covers seven economic sectors: energy, transport, construction, waste management and emissions capture, manufacturing, information and communication technologies, and water supply and treatment. [1]

fullDescription: Paraguay is building its first Green Taxonomy — a classification system for economic activities considered environmentally sustainable, intended to mobilise additional financial resources through a transparent, standardised framework aligned with the country's environmental commitments. [1] The Ministry of Economy and Finance (MEF) and the Central Bank of Paraguay (BCP), with support from the Inter-American Development Bank, opened a public consultation from 21 October to 10 November 2024, including a Technical Discussion Week involving roughly 50 organisations and over 200 participants from banks, industry groups, civil society and government. [1][2]

The consultation sought feedback on the taxonomy's structure, environmental objectives, economic sectors and activities, and substantial-contribution and compliance criteria. No confirmed finalised or officially published edition has been identified since that consultation closed, so the framework should currently be treated as under development rather than in force. [1][2]

fullDescription: The Superintendencia del Mercado de Valores (SIMV) and the Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources (MMARN) launched the Dominican Republic's Green Taxonomy with technical support from the International Finance Corporation (IFC), a classification instrument allowing investors, companies and other market participants to identify investment opportunities aligned with the country's environmental objectives. [1][2]

The Taxonomy is structured around six environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, water and water resources (a distinctive 'Blue' component, a pioneer in the region), ecosystems and biodiversity, pollution prevention, and circular economy. [1]

In its first stage it classifies activities across seven economic sectors — energy, waste management and emissions capture, construction, transport, water supply and treatment, ICT, and industry — with a broader nine-sector concept also covering disaster management and environmental protection/restoration. Its explicit water-resources ('Blue') objective is a notable innovation: the first taxonomy in the Americas, and one without global precedent, to give water resources this prominence as a primary objective. [1][2]

fullDescription: On 11 April 2022, Colombia officially launched its Green Taxonomy, making it the first country in the Americas to publish one. [1][2] It is an inter-institutional effort led by the Ministry of Finance and Public Credit and the Financial Superintendence, working alongside the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development, the National Planning Department and the National Administrative Department of Statistics within a Green Taxonomy Working Group.

The Taxonomy establishes a classification system for economic activities and assets that contribute to the country's environmental objectives — climate change mitigation and adaptation, conservation of ecosystems and biodiversity, water management, soil management, circular economy, and pollution prevention and control — with the purpose of promoting sustainable financing across the national territory. [1][3]

The first phase addresses the climate change mitigation objective across seven economic sectors, with a particular emphasis on land use: forestry, agriculture and livestock activities alone are responsible for 59% of Colombia's greenhouse gas emissions, making them a priority focus of the framework. [1][2]

fullDescription: Costa Rica launched its Sustainable Finance Taxonomy on 14 August 2024, following working tables involving more than 300 experts from over 180 public- and private-sector organisations, NGOs and academic institutions who evaluated economic activities and their technical criteria. [1][2]

The first phase prioritises two environmental objectives — climate change mitigation and climate change adaptation — across eight economic sectors including electricity, gas, steam and air-conditioning supply, construction, transport and manufacturing. For each sector, the Taxonomy identifies specific economic activities, assets and investment-project categories that show a substantial contribution to the prioritised objectives according to scientific criteria. [1][2]

Aligned activities must meet substantial-contribution criteria for at least one environmental objective without negatively affecting others, while complying with minimum human-rights and labour standards. The Taxonomy was designed to be interoperable with other jurisdictions' frameworks, including the EU Taxonomy. [2]

fullDescription: Panama's Sustainable Finance Taxonomy was launched in 2023, becoming the third country in the Americas to publish one, after Colombia and Mexico, and the first in Central America. [1] It aims to boost effective capital mobilisation toward investments considered strategic for transitioning to a sustainable, resilient and inclusive national economy, giving real-economy and financial-sector actors a reference framework for identifying economic activities and investments that contribute to the country's environmental and social objectives. [1][2]

The Taxonomy establishes eligibility criteria for economic activities grouped into eleven economic sectors: land use (livestock, agriculture and forestry), transport, construction, waste management and emissions capture, manufacturing, information and communication technologies, water supply and treatment, electricity/gas/steam and air conditioning, and financial and insurance activities. [1][2]

Each activity has its own eligibility criteria determining its substantial contribution toward the defined environmental objectives and its compliance requirements, ensuring it does not cause significant harm to other environmental objectives or generate negative social impact. Metrics and thresholds (quantitative and qualitative) were established for activities substantially contributing to climate change mitigation and adaptation. [2]

fullDescription: Chile's Ministry of Finance has led work since 2021, in collaboration with the Central Bank, the Financial Market Commission (CMF), the Pension Superintendency and the Ministry of the Environment, to develop a Taxonomy for Environmentally Sustainable Economic Activities, based on international best practices. [1][2]

Through 2024 and 2025, work focused on developing Technical Selection Criteria (Criterios Técnicos de Selección) to determine whether an economic activity can be considered environmentally sustainable — a technical and participatory process carried out within the Public-Private Green Finance Table, involving public institutions, business groups, academic experts, multilateral organisations and civil society. A draft Taxonomy (Anteproyecto) was delivered at the end of 2024. [1][3]

The Taxonomy covers nine economic sectors: agriculture, livestock, forestry and fishing; mining and quarrying; manufacturing industries; electricity, gas, steam and air conditioning supply; water supply, wastewater and waste management and remediation; construction; transport and storage; information and communications; and real estate activities. It aims to guide public and private investment decisions, improve transparency, and align the financial and productive sectors with the country's environmental objectives. [1][3]

In December 2025 the federal government announced two years of seed funding for a new governing council to finalise voluntary 'Made-in-Canada' sustainable investment guidelines, targeting three priority sectors (electricity, transportation, buildings, agriculture/forestry, manufacturing, extractives) by end of 2026 and further sectors by fall 2027. CAN.regulator: Department of Finance Canada / Sustainable Finance Action Council
fullDescription: Canada does not yet have a finalised sustainable finance taxonomy but is actively developing one — the 'Made-in-Canada' sustainable investment guidelines — as a voluntary, arm's-length market tool. The Sustainable Finance Action Council (SFAC), whose mandate concluded in March 2024, endorsed a Taxonomy Roadmap Report in 2022 with 10 recommendations for how such a framework should be built. [1]

In December 2025 the federal government announced two years of seed funding and selected the Canadian Climate Institute, working with Business Future Pathways, to lead development through a new governing Taxonomy Council. The guidelines are intended to be science-based and technology-neutral, screening out investments that could cause significant harm, and will include a distinct 'transition' category to identify and boost funding for scientifically credible pathways to rapidly decarbonise Canada's emissions-intensive sectors — going beyond a simple green/non-green binary. [2][3]

Development is initially focused on six priority sectors: electricity, transportation, buildings, agriculture and forestry, manufacturing, and extractives (including mineral extraction/processing and natural gas). The Taxonomy Council is expected to finalise guidelines for three priority sectors by the end of 2026 and three more by autumn 2027, with the framework designed to be broadly compatible with other major international science-based taxonomies. [2][3]

fullDescription: Ecuador does not yet have a finalised national green taxonomy. The Ministry of Economy and Finance, the Ministry of Environment and the National Planning Secretariat formalised a Sustainable Finance Table (Mesa de Finanzas Sostenibles) in October 2024, tasked with developing a green/social/sustainable taxonomy for the country. [1]

In December 2025, with support from the Global Green Growth Institute (GGGI), Ecuador published its Roadmap for the Green Taxonomy (Hoja de Ruta) — a guiding instrument establishing the conceptual and institutional foundations for building the national taxonomy, rather than the taxonomy's technical criteria themselves. [1][2]

Separately, the Association of Private Banks of Ecuador (ASOBANCA) began developing its own green taxonomy for member banks in 2023, implementing it from September 2024 alongside an Environmental and Social Risk Platform — a voluntary, industry-level classification effort distinct from the government's national roadmap process. [2]

The strategy is organised around five pillars covering both financial and non-financial aspects: the regulatory framework; a common language and taxonomy; generation of information, transparency and reporting (indicators and data analysis); incentives; and sustainable financial instruments. Within the 'common language and taxonomy' pillar, initial taxonomy development is focused on three sectors: agriculture, livestock and fishing; energy; and mining — intended as the first step toward reliable, comparable sustainability data across investments and actors in Argentina. Rather than an activity-based taxonomy, Uruguay's most prominent sustainable-finance instrument to date is its Sovereign Sustainability-Linked Bond (SSLB) — first issued in October 2022 — which directly links the country's cost of sovereign borrowing to outcome-based national targets under the Paris Agreement, including greenhouse-gas emissions intensity and native forest area, rather than classifying individual economic activities as green or not.

fullDescription: Guatemala has launched two voluntary classification tools via the Consejo Consultivo de Finanzas Sostenibles (Sustainable Finance Advisory Council), working with IDB Invest and CentraRSE (a coalition of 120 companies promoting corporate social responsibility): a Social Finance Taxonomy and a Voluntary Green Finance Taxonomy. Neither is yet a mandatory, economy-wide framework. [1][2]

The Social Finance Taxonomy, launched in 2024, was the first of its kind in Latin America — a classification system identifying investments with positive social impact, including projects that strengthen the competitiveness of micro, small and medium enterprises (with particular emphasis on those led by women), improve access to financing for indigenous populations and other vulnerable groups, and attract impact capital via thematic bonds. [2]

Alongside it, the Voluntary Green Finance Taxonomy covers renewable energy, energy efficiency, clean transport, sustainable construction, circular economy and water management. Both taxonomies form part of a renewed Sustainable Finance Protocol (originally signed in 2022), and Guatemala otherwise has no economy-wide mandatory ESG reporting regime beyond its Framework Climate Change Act. [1][2]

fullDescription: The United States has no federal green or sustainable finance taxonomy, and none is currently planned. [1] Rather than a centralized activity-classification system, U.S. green finance operates within a disclosure-based, principles-driven regulatory environment shaped by federal securities law, which emphasises materiality and enforcement against misleading claims rather than defining in advance which activities count as 'green'. [1]

In March 2024 the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) adopted climate-related disclosure rules requiring public companies to report climate risks that could affect investors' decisions — but the rules were almost immediately challenged in litigation and have been under a voluntary stay pending judicial review since 2025. [1][2] In the continued absence of a federal taxonomy, market-driven standards from organisations such as the Climate Bonds Initiative, together with tax-incentive mechanisms like the Inflation Reduction Act's transferable clean energy tax credits, function as de facto reference points for green finance activity. [1][3]

Financial Services Agency (FSA) — "Basic Guidelines on Climate Transition Finance" Announced

JPN.regulator: No national taxonomy issued; Financial Services Agency (FSA), METI and Ministry of the Environment jointly issue transition-finance guidance
fullDescription: Japan has not adopted a formal green or sustainable finance taxonomy comparable to the EU's or Korea's. Instead, its Financial Services Agency (FSA), Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) and Ministry of the Environment jointly published Basic Guidelines on Climate Transition Finance in May 2021 (updated in a 2025 edition) — a principles-based framework with metrics guidance but no binding technical thresholds, rather than a taxonomy with a fixed activities list. [1][2]

These guidelines are aimed at supporting 'transition finance' — financing decarbonisation in hard-to-abate sectors — in support of Japan's 2050 carbon-neutrality target, and are complemented by sector-specific technology roadmaps rather than a single classification system. [1] Most recently, on 8 January 2026 the FSA published a report from its Working Group on Disclosure and Assurance of Sustainability-related Financial Information, alongside a Japanese roadmap for sustainability disclosure and assurance — continuing to build out disclosure infrastructure without introducing a formal activities taxonomy. [3]

officialDocuments.title: UNEP FI / UNDP — Common Framework of Sustainable Finance Taxonomies for Latin America and the Caribbean

overlays.name: LAC Common Framework of Sustainable Finance Taxonomies
overlays.scope: Regional guidance (non-binding) for Latin America & the Caribbean

fullDescription: Nicaragua has not published a national green or sustainable finance taxonomy. [1] Along with other Latin American and Caribbean countries without their own frameworks, it can draw on the Common Framework of Sustainable Finance Taxonomies for Latin America and the Caribbean — a voluntary regional reference developed by UNEP FI, UNDP and partner institutions in 2023 to help countries design taxonomies that are interoperable with each other and with major international frameworks. [1][2]
